metadata.title: Why crowded features arrange themselves into perfect pentagons
metadata.topic: The geometry features adopt when forced to share dimensions
metadata.purpose: Under a norm-and-interference cost, minimizing the worst pairwise overlap is a packing problem whose optima are uniform polytopes — antipodal pairs, triangles, pentagons — the same regular arrangements as Thomson's problem.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: vectors, dot product as interference, dimensionality
metadata.exclusions: more-features-than-neurons capacity story (top-level carded), Thomson-problem proofs, higher-dimensional polytope enumeration
metadata.visual_object: A 2-D plane where feature vectors, initialized as a random scatter, rotate and relax into a regular pentagon.
metadata.hook: When a network has fewer dimensions than features, the features don't crowd in randomly — they snap into regular geometric shapes.

beats.narration_text: Five equally-important features forced through a 2-D bottleneck at high sparsity settle as a regular pentagon — five unit vectors 72° apart — not a random scatter.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Packing more vectors than dimensions should produce messy overlap; instead the trained network lands on evenly-spaced regular polygons; why does interference minimize into symmetry?
When a network has fewer dimensions than features, the features don't crowd in randomly — they snap into regular geometric shapes.

Under a norm-and-interference cost, minimizing the worst pairwise overlap is a packing problem whose optima are uniform polytopes — antipodal pairs, triangles, pentagons — the same regular arrangements as Thomson's problem.
beats.visual: A 2-D plane where feature vectors, initialized as a random scatter, rotate and relax into a regular pentagon.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: 5 features, 2 hidden dims, sparsity 0.9 → vectors relax to 0°, 72°, 144°, 216°, 288°; drop to 4 features and it becomes a square.

Under a norm-and-interference cost, minimizing the worst pairwise overlap is a packing problem whose optima are uniform polytopes — antipodal pairs, triangles, pentagons — the same regular arrangements as Thomson's problem.
